title: Plastic waste per capita imports vs. exports
caption:
Plastic waste per capita imports vs. exports, 2024
Imports per person (tonnes; plotted on a logarithmic axis)

The scatter plot maps the relationship between plastic waste exports and imports per person for various countries in 2024, with both axes plotted on a logarithmic scale ranging from 0.000001 to over 10 tonnes. Each point represents a country, color-coded by income group: purple for low-income, pink for lower-middle-income, light green for upper-middle-income, and dark green for high-income countries. A diagonal line indicates where imports equal exports. High-income nations generally occupy the upper-right quadrant, showing high volumes of both trade flows. Austria and Norway appear at the extreme top right, with exports and imports both exceeding 10 tonnes per person. Canada and Bulgaria also sit high on the vertical axis, indicating imports around 10 tonnes per person against exports between 1 and 10 tonnes. Conversely, many low-income and lower-middle-income countries cluster in the bottom-left corner. Nepal, Burundi, and Cote d'Ivoire register values near the bottom of the scale, with both imports and exports below 0.0001 tonnes per person. Some nations show distinct imbalances; the Bahamas and Malaysia have imports exceeding 10 tonnes per person while exports remain lower, around 0.1 to 1 tonne. In contrast, Iceland and Barbados show exports near 10 tonnes per person but imports below 0.01 tonnes. The distribution shows that higher income groups tend to have higher per capita trade volumes in plastic waste, while the lowest values are concentrated among low-income and lower-middle-income economies. The data points span several orders of magnitude, illustrating the vast differences in plastic waste trade intensity across the global economy.
